title: Genetic Drift

records.question: What makes drift different from selection?
records.answer_summary: Drift changes allele frequencies by random sampling. Selection changes frequencies because heritable variants differ in survival or reproduction.
records.verification_prompt: Ask whether the evidence shows consistent directional advantage or strong variation among repeated runs.
records.misconception_guard: Do not treat any increase in frequency as proof of selective advantage.
records.didactopus_prompt_seed: State one observation that fits drift and one that would make selection more plausible.

records.question: Why is drift easier to see in small populations?
records.answer_summary: Sampling error has a larger effect when fewer individuals contribute to the next generation, so allele frequencies can swing more sharply.
records.verification_prompt: Compare repeated small-population and large-population runs from the same starting frequency.
records.misconception_guard: Do not describe drift as weak selection; chance sampling is a different mechanism.
records.didactopus_prompt_seed: Explain why two small-population runs with the same starting settings can end differently.

records.question: What does fixation mean?
records.answer_summary: Fixation means one allele reaches frequency 1.0 in the population, while alternatives are lost.
records.verification_prompt: Identify the final population frequency and whether competing alleles remain.
records.misconception_guard: Do not assume fixation proves the fixed allele was better adapted.
records.didactopus_prompt_seed: Describe a drift-only path to fixation without using the language of benefit or design.

records.question: Why compare repeated runs?
records.answer_summary: Repeated runs show whether a result is consistently directional or varies strongly, which helps distinguish more stable selective pressure from drift-dominated change.
records.verification_prompt: Compare multiple runs under the same settings before naming the mechanism.
records.misconception_guard: A single dramatic run is not enough to settle the mechanism.
records.didactopus_prompt_seed: Rewrite the first explanation after seeing four more runs under the same conditions.

records.question: Can drift and selection both matter?
records.answer_summary: Yes. Small populations can show noisy outcomes even when selection is present, and weak selection can be hard to distinguish from drift without more evidence.
records.verification_prompt: Ask whether the pattern remains after repeated runs or stronger evidence of fitness differences is added.
records.misconception_guard: Do not force every case into a drift-only or selection-only explanation when mixed mechanisms are plausible.
records.didactopus_prompt_seed: Name what extra evidence would help sort out a mixed drift-selection explanation.

citegeist_source_slots.needed_for: Foundational treatments of drift, sampling, and population size
candidate_queries: Wright 1931 evolution in Mendelian populations genetic drift
Sewall Wright genetic drift population size fixation

citegeist_source_slots.needed_for: Neutral or near-neutral explanations relevant to drift interpretation
candidate_queries: Kimura 1968 evolutionary rate at molecular level neutral mutation
neutral theory drift fixation molecular evolution

doclift_use: Use this JSON as a fixture for extracting or validating scaffold-backed concept pages about population-genetic mechanisms.
groundrecall_use: Store rationale, pending source-slot work, and later revisions as durable notes so related Notebook concepts can reuse the distinction between drift and selection.
next_review_steps: Backfill reviewed sources for Wright and later drift or neutral-theory explanations.
